questions.question: What is the evidence grade for social support as a buffer against relationship stress?

options.text: WEAK—relationships don't affect stress
STRONG—the 2025 APA meta-analysis (604 studies) found perceived social support most strongly associated with mental health (r+=0.35); relationship quality is a primary stress buffer or amplifier
MODERATE—only anecdotal evidence exists
No evidence connects relationships to stress

questions.explanation: Social support has STRONG meta-analytic evidence (2025 APA review, 604 studies, 1,014 effect sizes) showing perceived social support is most strongly associated with better mental health (r+=0.35) and better work performance (r+=0.37). Relationship quality matters enormously for stress: supportive relationships buffer stress through emotional validation, practical help, and sense of belonging; conversely, conflictual or abusive relationships are major stress amplifiers. The research brief identifies 'social stress' as uniquely activating both HPA axis and inflammatory pathways, while supportive relationships buffer stress responses. This makes relationship quality one of the most impactful factors in stress resilience.

questions.question: How does attachment theory relate to relationship stress and stress resilience?

options.text: Attachment theory is only relevant to parent-child relationships

Attachment patterns (secure, anxious, avoidant) shape how people seek and receive support under stress—secure attachment predicts better stress buffering from relationships; insecure patterns create relationship stress itself
Attachment has no connection to adult stress
Only therapy, not relationships, affects stress

questions.explanation: Attachment theory describes how early relationship experiences shape internal working models of self and others that persist into adulthood. Secure attachment (feeling worthy of love, trusting others are available) predicts better stress resilience because securely attached people seek support effectively, accept help, maintain stable relationships, and use social connection as a stress buffer. Anxious attachment (fear of abandonment, hyperactivation of attachment system) can create relationship stress through excessive reassurance-seeking and conflict. Avoidant attachment (discomfort with closeness, deactivation of attachment) limits stress buffering by preventing effective support-seeking. Understanding attachment patterns helps people recognize relationship patterns that either buffer or amplify stress.

questions.question: What is the evidence-based relationship between communication skills and relationship stress?

options.text: Communication skills have no effect on relationship stress

MODERATE to STRONG evidence—assertive communication (expressing needs while respecting others) is associated with lower stress and better relationship satisfaction; poor communication escalates conflict
Only couples therapy, not individual skills, affects relationship stress
Communication makes relationships worse

questions.explanation: Communication skills training is a core component of evidence-based stress management and relationship interventions. Assertive communication (expressing needs clearly while respecting others' boundaries) is associated with lower stress, better mental health, and higher relationship satisfaction compared to passive (suppressing needs) or aggressive (violating others' boundaries) styles. Poor communication creates stress through: unmet needs (passive), escalating conflict (aggressive), chronic misunderstanding, and eroded relationship quality—which then reduces the stress-buffering capacity of the relationship. However, as covered in lesson 8, communication skills only work in relationships where both people operate in good faith—they cannot fix abusive or manipulative relationships.

questions.question: Why do interpersonal conflicts uniquely activate stress response systems?

options.text: Interpersonal conflicts don't affect stress any differently than other stressors
Social stress uniquely activates both HPA axis and inflammatory pathways—our nervous systems are particularly sensitive to social threat because connection is a fundamental human need
Only physical danger activates stress systems
Social conflicts have no biological effects

questions.explanation: The research brief explicitly notes that 'social stress uniquely activates both HPA axis and inflammatory pathways.' This makes evolutionary sense: humans are fundamentally social creatures whose survival historically depended on group membership. Our nervous systems are highly attuned to social threat—rejection, exclusion, conflict, loss of status—because these historically meant survival risk. Modern interpersonal conflicts activate these ancient systems, producing both cortisol elevation (HPA axis) and inflammatory markers (immune system activation). This explains why relationship stress often feels particularly intense and why chronic relationship conflict is a significant health risk factor. Conversely, supportive relationships buffer these stress responses.

questions.question: When does relationship stress require professional intervention rather than communication skills or stress management?

options.text: All relationship conflicts can be solved with better communication

Domestic violence, emotional/psychological abuse, coercive control, persistent violation of boundaries despite clear communication, or fear for safety—these require professional support and safety planning, not skill-building
Relationship problems never require professional help
Only physical abuse, not emotional abuse, requires intervention

questions.explanation: This is a critical safety consideration. Relationship stress crosses into territory requiring professional intervention when it involves: domestic violence (physical, sexual), emotional or psychological abuse (manipulation, degradation, isolation, gaslighting), coercive control (monitoring, restricting freedom, financial control), persistent violation of boundaries despite clear assertive communication (showing the issue is not communication skills but unwillingness to respect boundaries), or any situation where you fear for your physical or psychological safety. In these cases, communication skills training is insufficient and potentially dangerous—it may signal to an abuser that you're 'pushing back' and escalate danger. Appropriate resources include: domestic violence hotlines, safety planning with trained advocates, therapists specializing in trauma/abuse, legal support, and when safe, relationship exit planning.

title: Relationship Stress and Conflict Resolution - Quiz
